questions.question_text: Which statement explains why $-8$ is a rational number?

options.text: It can be written as $\frac{-8}{1}$, a fraction of two integers.
It is negative, and all negative numbers are rational.
options.why: Sign has nothing to do with it; a number is rational because it can be written as a fraction of integers.

options.text: It is even, and all even numbers are rational.
options.why: Being even is irrelevant; rationality is about being writable as $\frac{a}{b}$ with integers.

options.text: It cannot be written as a fraction, so it is rational.
options.why: This is back-to-front; rational numbers CAN be written as a fraction of integers.

questions.solution_text:
$-8=\frac{-8}{1}$, a fraction of two integers with denominator not $0$.
**Rational.**

options.text: It has two decimal places, and short decimals are rational.
options.why: The number of decimal places is not the reason; it is rational because it equals a fraction of integers.

options.text: It is less than $1$, and numbers below $1$ are rational.
options.why: Size below $1$ is irrelevant; for example $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}<1$ is irrational.

options.text: It rounds to $0$, so it is rational.
options.why: Rounding is irrelevant; rationality means it can be written as $\frac{a}{b}$.

questions.question_text: Which working shows that $85\%$ is rational?

options.why: A percentage is out of $100$; $85\%=\frac{85}{100}$, not $85$.

options.text: $85\%$ is rational just because it has a $\%$ sign.
options.why: The symbol alone is not a reason; it is rational because $85\%=\frac{85}{100}$.
To drop the $\%$ divide by $100$: $85\%=0.85$, not $8.5$.

questions.question_text: Which of these numbers is rational?

options.why: $\pi$ is a non-terminating, non-recurring decimal and cannot be written as a fraction.
$\sqrt{3}$ is a surd; it cannot be written as a fraction of integers.
Adding $1$ to the irrational $\sqrt{2}$ still cannot be written as a fraction.

questions.solution_text:
$0.\dot{2}=\frac{2}{9}$, a fraction of two integers.
$\pi$, $\sqrt{3}$ and $\sqrt{2}+1$ cannot be written as a fraction.
$0.\dot{2}$ is **rational**.

questions.question_text: Which list contains only rational numbers?

options.why: $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational, so this list is not all rational.
$\pi$ is irrational, so this list is not all rational.
$\sqrt{5}$ is irrational, so this list is not all rational.

questions.solution_text:
$\frac{3}{4}$ is a fraction, $0.6=\frac{3}{5}$, and $25\%=\frac{1}{4}$ — all fractions of integers.
Every other list contains a surd or $\pi$, which are irrational.
The list $\frac{3}{4},\ 0.6,\ 25\%$ is **all rational**.

questions.question_text: $0.\dot{7}$ has digits that never stop. Which statement is correct?

options.text: It is irrational, because its digits never stop.
options.why: Never-stopping digits can still repeat; a repeating decimal equals a fraction, so it is rational.

options.text: It is irrational, because it is a decimal, not a fraction.
options.why: Every recurring decimal can be rewritten as a fraction, e.g. $0.\dot{7}=\frac{7}{9}$.

options.text: It is neither rational nor irrational.
options.why: Every real number is one or the other; $0.\dot{7}$ is rational.

questions.solution_text:
A recurring decimal repeats, so it can be written as a fraction: $0.\dot{7}=\frac{7}{9}$.
Because it equals a fraction of two integers it is **rational**.
